item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm just really happy that scp 1976 is a class of '76 one. I like it when something is done with the number, even though it doesn't really affect anything here apart from being kind of cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to admit, while the SCP in itself is good and creepy, the thing that freaks me out the most is that I'm from Huntington WV. I could totally picture some anomalous stuff going down there. It really gives me the shivers. +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

With this being a Class of '76 skip, I'm going to try and keep it in the family and link to other groups in this often overlooked Group of Interest. Both Remembrance and SCP-814 would work wonderfully, as they can be linked to inline, and could use a little more attention themselves, and most importantly are Class of '76 articles. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

Text is believed to have been created by Alan ██████, a former schoolteacher who had died of a brain tumor 2 months prior to SCP-1976's recovery. Investigation into connections between this individual and possible groups of interest have not yielded substantial evidence. It is possible that Mr. ██████ is connected to the Syncope Symphony.

To this:

Text is believed to have been created by Alan ██████, a former schoolteacher who had died of a brain tumor 2 months prior to SCP-1976's recovery. Investigation into connections between this individual and possible groups of interest have not yielded substantial evidence. It is possible that Mr. ██████ is connected to the Syncope Symphony.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohh now I get where I was wrong

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope. The entity is the one in the vegetative state.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…responsible for its creation, an adult male named ████ ██████, in a persistent vegetative state…

…created by ████ ██████, who had died of a brain tumor 2 months prior to…

Contradictory description?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something's bugging me about this… why have the dad's name censored in the documentation if it's uncensored in the note he left behind?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SUPERIOR ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Addendum is good. But the rest… Eh. Conceptually, this just isn't all that interesting. I've seen this kind of thing before quite a lot. I really wish there was more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really quite good, compounded by the fact that we really aren't even aware of what this thing is, or what it actually looks like. A homunculus at its most disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the way the addendum is written. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this idea and the addendum, but I felt the presentation was a bit convoluted. The particulars— the people who can be affected seem sort of awkward or too specific as-written. Will come back to this later to see if a reread improves it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, rereading the tag I realized that it is misused. Removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Obviously because it's such a moving piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can anyone explain why the 'telekinetic' tag? o.o
Other than that, quite nice. Not fully interesting per-se, but the story behind it, created by a random someone, and failed it's objective~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the addendum that does it for me, honestly. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like that this was created and more or less has the effect it was intended to have, if a bit overboard. However, I don't think it's really all that interesting. It's "Luke, I am your father" in SCP form, to put it cynically.

item.title: Erm...
item.content:encoded:
If a pubescent human subject

Jess is 8, and Liz just turned 4.

When I showed Liz and Jess the thing…they just asked where their dad was.

I'm really confused. These kids are nowhere near pubescent, so why didn't they recognize that the "emergency worker" dropping off this bizarre simulacrum was their father?

Also, I'm pretty sure this isn't "telekinetic".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The middle of the addendum sounds like he worked with some sort of SCP. I think it would be even better if the brain tumor made him into a limited reality bender, but this is still good enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, it just sort of sounds a bit "off". Guess cause I'm just too used to the old ways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the addendum would be stronger without the second and third paragraphs, but author's choice. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Technically, an amnesiac is one who suffers from memory loss while an amnestic is something that causes it.

There's been a fairly recent effort to switch to the more correct term.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah. That leads me to believe that what ever he used was used up in the process. Either way, i love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Investigation into connections between this individual and possible groups of interest have not yielded substantial evidence."

from the addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. It just gets you. The only thing is what about the item he claimed to have used? I figure that would be something the foundation would look for and after it's mentioned in the letter, it's forgotten.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dammit, take your tear soaked upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was told that "Amnestic" is a more correct term.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you confused "amnestic" with "amnesiac".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely interesting. Superficially similar to other perception-altering humanoid SCPs, but unique enough that I'm willing to give it an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed the euclid thing, that was an oversight on my part

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting concept, nice and weird. Upvoted.

Question, though. Is there a reason that it's in the Euclid wing as a Safe object? And couldn't you just put curtains or something around the skip instead of making everyone entering its chamber wear goggles?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All aboard the one way train to feelstown!
